coredata.dc:title: Unveiling the effect law of carbon dots with polyfunctional groups on interface structure and ion migration in polymer electrolytes for solid lithium battery
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Huaxin
Xu, Laiqiang
Zhu, Fangjun
Luo, Dingzhong
Zhang, Yi
Deng, Wentao
Zou, Guoqiang
Hou, Hongshuai
Ji, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: Solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) has been considered as promising candidate electrolyte for solid-state lithium metal battery. However, the low ion conductivity and poor interface stability seriously hinder the development of SPE. Introducing the functional filler is a commonly used and effective approach to solving these issues. The poor compatibility and ambiguous mechanism of action are currently the main challenges. In this study, we design and synthesize carbon dots (NSFCDs) with quaternary functional groups, which are introduced into the SPE in the polymerization process of hybrid polymer electrolyte (HPE). The detailed effect law of polyfunctional groups on the polymer chain structure, interface structure of electrolyte/electrode and ion migration is systematically revealed. The dense polymer network endows HPE with enhanced mechanical properties. Through the regulation of polymer segments, the coordination environment of lithium ions is altered, promoting ion transport. The solid electrolyte interface (SEI) induced by NSFCDs and lithium salt anions effectively improves the morphology of lithium ion deposition and alleviates volume strain. As a result, the HPE based on polyethylene oxide (PEO) constructed by free radical polymerization of NSFCDs and polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA) exhibits excellent comprehensive performance, the assembled Li symmetric battery can cycle stably for 4000 h.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid polymer electrolyte
Carbon dots
Polymer construction
Interface regulation
Solid-state lithium battery

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52074359, 22379165, U21A20284, U22B2069), Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2021RC3014), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University (2023ZZTS0068) and Hunan Provincial Innovation Foundation for Postgraduate (CX20230153).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic diagram of the formation of NSFCDs. b, c) 1H NMR (b) and 13C NMR (c) spectra of NSFCDs. d) TEM image of NSFCDs. e) HRTEM image of NSFCDs.
a) Schematic diagram of cross-linking between NSFCDs and PEGDA under UV irradiation. b) FTIR of HPE before and after UV irradiation. c) 13C NMR of HPE before and after UV irradiation.

Simulation results of lithium ion migration trajectory for a) SPE, b) PDA and c) PDACDs. Mean-azimuth shift curves for d) SPE, e) PDA and f) PDACDs in the simulation box systems. g) 7Li NMR results of electrolytes. h) The initial circle overpotential of Li/Cu batteries assembled with SPE, PDA and PDACDs. i) Tafel curves of Li/Li symmetric batteries assembled with SPE, PDA and PDACDs.
a) Cycling performance of Li symmetric batteries at 0.1 mA·cm−1 at 60 °C. b) O 1 s and S 2p spectra of electrolytes by XPS sputter etching.

AFM images of a) SPE, b) PDA and c) PDACDs. d) SEM images of Li metal surface before and after cycling. e) Cycle performance of Li | LFP batteries at 60 °C. f) Rate performance of Li | LFP batteries at 60 °C.

head.ce:title: Unveiling the effect law of carbon dots with polyfunctional groups on interface structure and ion migration in polymer electrolytes for solid lithium battery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083 China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha, 410004 (China).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) has been considered as promising candidate electrolyte for solid-state lithium metal battery. However, the low ion conductivity and poor interface stability seriously hinder the development of SPE. Introducing the functional filler is a commonly used and effective approach to solving these issues. The poor compatibility and ambiguous mechanism of action are currently the main challenges. In this study, we design and synthesize carbon dots (NSFCDs) with quaternary functional groups, which are introduced into the SPE in the polymerization process of hybrid polymer electrolyte (HPE). The detailed effect law of polyfunctional groups on the polymer chain structure, interface structure of electrolyte/electrode and ion migration is systematically revealed. The dense polymer network endows HPE with enhanced mechanical properties. Through the regulation of polymer segments, the coordination environment of lithium ions is altered, promoting ion transport. The solid electrolyte interface (SEI) induced by NSFCDs and lithium salt anions effectively improves the morphology of lithium ion deposition and alleviates volume strain. As a result, the HPE based on polyethylene oxide (PEO) constructed by free radical polymerization of NSFCDs and polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA) exhibits excellent comprehensive performance, the assembled Li symmetric battery can cycle stably for 4000 h.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A functional filler of carbon dots equipped with polyfunctional groups was designed and synthesized to improve the electrochemical performance of solid polymer electrolyte. A Dense polymer network was constructed and effectively improved the mechanical properties of the polymer matrix. The lithium ion coordination environment has been improved and the rational composition of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) was regulated, effectively alleviating the growth of lithium metal dendrites and volume strain. The prepared hybrid polymer electrolyte exhibited excellent properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium ion batteries have developed rapidly in fields such as consumer electronics and electric vehicles due to their high energy density and long cycle life [1]. At present, the actual energy density of commercialized lithium-ion batteries based on graphite anodes is gradually approaching its theoretical limit value [2], and there is an urgent need to develop high-capacity electrode materials to further improve the energy density of secondary batteries [3–5]. Lithium metal anodes have extremely high theoretical specific capacity (3860 mAh g−1), the most negative electrochemical potential (-3.04 V, relative to standard hydrogen electrodes), and extremely low density (0.59 g cm−3), making them the most ideal negative electrode material for lithium batteries [6]. However, during the charging and discharging process, due to the uneven deposition of lithium ions, dendrites are easily formed on the surface of metallic lithium, especially in conventional liquid electrolytes. Lithium dendrites continue to grow and eventually penetrate the separator, leading to battery short circuits and safety issues, seriously restricting the practicality of lithium metal batteries [7,8]. As an emerging battery technology, all solid-state lithium batteries utilize noncombustible inorganic compounds or polymer solid electrolytes (SPE) instead of flammable electrolytes, which can effectively improve the safety of high energy density batteries [9]. The introduction of solid-state electrolytes can broaden the selection space for high specific capacity positive and negative electrode materials [10], which is conducive to further improving the specific energy of the battery [11]. SPE are typically composed of a polymer matrix and lithium salts as solutes [12]. Compared with inorganic solid electrolytes, which are fragile, expensive, and difficult in mass production, SPE are lightweight, flexible, and scalable. As a result, they exhibit better compatibility with electrodes, leading to improved efficiency in solid-state battery manufacturing. However, limitations arise due to the low ionic conductivity of polymers at room temperature, poor stability at high voltage and unsatisfactory interface stability, restricting their development potential [13,14]. To meet the performance requirements of ideal solid electrolytes used in solid-state batteries, incorporating fillers to construct composite electrolytes is an effective strategy for enhancing the comprehensive performance of SPE [15–19].

Ever since the unveiling of quantum dots, extensive research and application efforts have been devoted to this material across various fields. It stands as an exemplary manifestation of the fusion between nanotechnology and quantum mechanics, earning it the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2023. Carbon dots (CDs) also play an indispensable role as one branch of quantum dots. CDs are widely defined as carbon nanoparticles with a size less than 10 nm. They are widely applied in fields such as cell imaging and fluorescence sensing due to their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and photoluminescence.[20] CDs contain abundant organic functional groups on their surface, which have excellent compatibility with polymers and are ideal fillers for polymer electrolytes [21–23]. In recent years, polymer electrolyte manufacturing processes based on free radical polymerization have been continuously proposed [24–26], which can design polymer structures from monomers and has broad design space.

Here, we designed and synthesized carbon dots with four functional groups (O, N, S and F), and utilized its surface unsaturated double bonds and PEGDA for free radical polymerization to construct a dense polymer network. The functional attributes of diverse elements are amalgamated in NSFCDs. Both N and S exhibit capabilities in engaging in lithium ion transport and interface regulation, while F primarily contributes to the regulation of interface components. The cross-linked polymer designed from this strategy has satisfactory mechanical properties and can effectively tolerate the volume strain of lithium metal during cycling. By regulating the PEO matrix in the lithium ion coordination environment, the lithium ion transport process is promoted. The constructed HPE can derive reasonable solid electrolyte interface (SEI) components to guide the uniform deposition of lithium ions. Furthermore, as assembled Li symmetric batteries, a remarkable long-term cycle stability of 4000 h at a current density of 0.1 mA cm−2 was achieved. And the assembled solid-state lithium metal battery exhibits excellent electrochemical performance.

NSFCDs were synthesized according to the method described in the Supporting Information. The formation of NSFCDs may include the following process. Growing carbon chains were formed as acetaldehyde molecules undergo cross-linking and polymerization through an aldol condensation reaction. Extended peptide chains were generated through dehydration and condensation of L-cysteine molecules. Intermolecular aldol condensation reaction cannot conduct with p-fluorobenzaldehyde due to the absence of alpha hydrogen, so the two types of intermolecular reactions mentioned above mainly occurred in the early stages of the reaction. The produced intermediate can crosslink with p-fluorobenzaldehyde, leading to various crosslinking reactions later. In addition, the hydroxyl groups introduced during cross-linking and the carboxyl groups generated by the Cannizzaro reaction of aldehydes can be dehydrated and condensed with L-cysteine. Through these complex cross-linking processes, the three reactants ultimately formed multi-element doped NSFCDs under strong alkaline conditions. Based on this, we proposed several possible reaction products and their formation processes in Fig. 1a.

High resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) was used to verify this process. In the fragment ion peak of HRMS (Figure S1a), the molecular weights of several intermediate products consist with those shown in Fig. 1a (boxed in red) can be found. The molecular weight of 220.1 may attribute to intermediate products in box 1, similarly, the peak at 274.3 and 338.1 may belong to intermediate products in box 2 and 3, respectively. It was challenging to find other possible intermediate products corresponding to the spectral peaks in Figure S1a-c, which may be due to the impossibility for some structures to produce stable fragment ions that can be captured by HRMS. However, there was an interesting pattern in these peaks, where the difference in the mass of fragments between some fragment ion peaks happened to be 18 or 26, which corresponded to the molecular weight of H2O and -C2H2-. This further proved the existence of dehydration reactions or chain growth initiated by acetaldehyde.

The synthesis strategy of CDs based on aldol condensation reaction generally achieve satisfactory yield. Above 3 g of NSFCDs can be synthesized in a single batch in this work (Figure S2), which presented light yellow under visible light. Photoluminescence is an important property of CDs, the fluorescence emission spectra of NSFCDs under different wavelengths of excitation light are shown in Figure S1d. Fluorescence emission of NSFCDs can be observed between 440 ∼ 660 nm, especially, the maximum intensity of yellow fluorescence emission can be achieved under the wavelength of 500 nm. The optical properties of CDs are closely related to their surface functionalization states. Fig. 1b-c displayed the 1H and 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra of NSFCDs, respectively. Due to the diverse cross-linking process of NSFCDs, the peaks corresponding to some functional groups exist in the form of broad peaks and multiple peaks in the NMR spectrum. In 1H NMR spectra, the peaks appeared at 10.21, 9.99 ppm correspond to -COOH and -CHO, respectively. The broad peaks located at 8.39 ∼ 6.36 ppm were attributed to aromatic hydrogen, which mainly derived from p-fluorobenzaldehyde. The multiple peaks presented at 5.53 ∼ 4.41 ppm and 1.45 ∼ 0.84 ppm belonged to H connected to sp2 C and sp3 C on different carbon chain skeletons. Alkyl hydrogen directly connected to double bonds appeared at 2.67 ∼ 1.91 ppm, and -CH2O- corresponded to peaks at 3.36 ppm. In 13C NMR, carbonyl C, sp2 C, and C-H corresponded to peaks located at 165.5 ∼ 159.3 ppm, 133.2 ∼ 114.0 ppm and 80.7 ∼ 76.7 ppm, respectively. More specific functionalization forms of carbon and hydrogen elements can be characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As plotted in Figure S3, the FTIR results indicated that a broad peak above 3116 cm−1 was observed, suggesting the presence of -OH and -NH-. The stretching vibrations of alkyl hydrogen and alkenyl hydrogen were located at around 3054 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1 ∼ 2856 cm−1. The broad peaks between 1747 cm−1 and 1455 cm−1 mainly attributed to C-O and CC on the straight chain, and several sharp peaks (1604 cm−1 and 1508 cm−1) corresponded to aromatic hydrogen. The existence of Ar-F (1226 cm−1) and C-N (1159 cm−1) can also be confirmed. The content of each element in NSFCDs was determined by XPS and summarized in Table S1 (Supporting Information). XPS confirmed the existence of these elements (Figure S4a). Peak fitting was performed on the high-resolution spectra of each element (Figure S4b-e). C 1 s can be fitted into five peaks, corresponding to C-C/CC, C-N/C-S, C-O, CO, O-CO and C-F bonds at 284.8, 285.6, 286.2, 286.6, 288.0 and 291.6 eV, respectively. Pyridinic, pyrrolic and graphitic N at 399.0, 400.2 and 401.5 eV can be fitted according to N 1 s spectra[27,28]. S element mainly existed in C-S (163.0, 164.2 eV) and C-SOx-C (166.0, 167.8 eV)[27], and F element can only exist as C-F (686.9 eV) on the benzene ring.

The XRD pattern of NSFCDs (Figure S5) showed a broad peak at ∼ 19°, due to the extremely small size of the carbon dots, they show amorphous structure on the macro level. The morphology and size distribution of NSFCDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fig. 1d showed that NSFCDs were uniformly dispersed in clusters, consisting of several single particles surrounding each other. The size of NSFCDs ranges from 1 to 4 nm, with an average size of 2.32 nm (Figure S6). The lattice fringes of NSFCDs can be clearly observed at high magnification (Fig. 1e), and the lattice spacing has been determined to be 0.2528 nm. Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was conducted to evaluate the thermal stability of NSFCDs (Figure S7). When the temperature rose to 90 °C, about 3 % of the mass loss may be due to the loss of trace bound water. As the temperature further increased, the functional groups on the surface began to lose. NSFCDs experienced rapid mass loss above 200 °C, corresponding to the decomposition of a large amount of oxygen-containing functional groups[29]. The transformation of the internal carbon nucleus structure mainly occurred over 400 °C, where the carbon atoms on the carbon chain may form more disordered aromatic clusters[30]. The results of TG indicated that NSFCDs are thermally stable in the subsequent operating environment.

Due to the abundant functional groups on the surface, NSFCDs can be well dispersed in anhydrous acetonitrile (Figure S8), providing assurance for the formation of uniform HPE membrane. Considering the presence of CC in NSFCDs, we proposed a crosslinking reaction process with PEGDA in the presence of photo-initiators ( Fig. 2a). PEGDA can undergo intermolecular crosslinking, forming longer chain segments. It can also crosslink with CC on the polymer segments in NSFCDs, generating more branched chains. During this process, NSFCDs acted as cross-linking centers and ultimately formed interwoven polymer networks. The HPE formed by PEGDA and PEO was denoted as PDA, whereas the HPE incorporating NSFCDs was labeled as PDACDs. SPE represented the traditional electrolyte composed of PEO and lithium salts. In order to evaluate the reaction of free radical polymerization of HPE before and after ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation, FTIR and 13C NMR measurements were conducted on the prepared HPE. As plotted in Fig. 2b, in the FTIR spectra of HPE before and after UV irradiation, there was no significant change in the stretching vibration band corresponding to CC (1650 cm−1), and even related absorption peaks were observed in SPE. From the FTIR results of PEO and LiTFSI, it can be inferred that this absorption peak originated from LiTFSI, thereby limiting the possibility of judging whether polymerization reaction occurred based on the change of CC absorption peak. Fortunately, we can still observe the bending vibration of =C-H (in plane bending vibration at 1405 cm−1 and out of plane bending vibration at 810 cm−1). These two vibration absorption peaks both disappeared after illumination, confirming the occurrence of polymerization reaction. In the 13C NMR spectra (Fig. 2c), the peaks corresponding to CC (132, 128 ppm) and the adjacent CO (166 ppm) bonds also disappeared after UV irradiation. In addition, the ether oxygen bonds with different chemical environments (68, 64 ppm) in PEGDA also merged into one peak (70 ppm) after UV irradiation, indicating that the disappearance of CC and the formation of new C-C have changed the symmetry of PEGDA, further explained the occurrence of polymerization reaction. Based on the dissolution experiments for all samples, crosslinked HPE only swelled in chloroform, while SPE and unpolymerized HPE can dissolve in it (Figure S9).

The properties of PEGDA polymerized with NSFCDs were studied by gel permeation chromatography (GPC). Elution curves of HPE samples under high angle light scattering device is displayed in Figure S10a. It can be observed that under the influence of NSFCDs, the peak of the elution curve of PDACDs shifted towards the direction of low molecular weight, indicating that PDACDs have a smaller average molecular weight compared to PDA, which can also be confirmed in the molecular weight distribution curves ( Fig. 3b, d). Information relevant to the flexibility of chain segments can be derived by plotting the logarithm of the intrinsic viscosity (IV) against the logarithm of molecular weight (Fig. 3a). According to Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (MHS) equation, two constants of HPE can be obtained after fitting the linear part of the curves. The value of MHS exponent for PDA (0.71) was bigger than that of PDACDs (0.47), suggesting that PDA was a more extended random coil compared to PDACDs[31]. The conformation of HPE can be evaluated by plotting the logarithm of the hydrodynamic radius (Rh) against the logarithm of molecular weight (Fig. 3c). Similarly, after fitting the curves, it can be observed that PDA (0.57) had a larger slope than PDACDs (0.50), indicating that PDACDs were approach to spherical polymer structures, while PDA tend to have a random coil conformation (Fig. 3e). The results of GPC declared that polymers with dense network and lower degree of polymerization were formed after the participation of NSFCDs in the crosslinking polymerization process. Compared to PDA, PDACDs had a higher degree of branching.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and confocal laser scanning microscopy were used to survey the changes in surface morphology of HPE before and after polymerization. It can be observed that SPE and HPE before polymerization were composed of polymer spherulites. Due to the introduction of PEGDA, the spherical spacing of HPE was larger than that of SPE (Figure S11a). In addition, PEGDA possessed repeating units consistent with PEO, larger spherulites were observed in PDA (Figure S11b), while PDACDs (Figure S11d) had slightly smaller spherulites than PDA due to the effect of NSFCDs. After polymerization, both PDA (Figure S11c) and PDACDs (Figure S11g) exhibited a flat and dense surface, and the boundaries of the spherulites became blurrier. The results of confocal laser scanning microscopy (Figure S11e, h) also showed the disappearance of spherulites after polymerization. NSFCDs exhibit bright yellow fluorescence under 458 nm excitation light. It is worth noting that the polymerized HPE exhibited reduced fluorescence intensity (Figure S11f, i), which was similarly evident in the alteration of color observed under visible light throughout the preparation process of the HPE (Figure S12). The attenuation of fluorescence intensity was attributed to the destruction of the conjugated structure of NSFCDs, further confirming the involvement of NSFCDs in the polymerization process. The XRD patterns (Figure S13a) indicated that the crosslinked HPE had lower crystallinity compared to SPE, which was attributed to the fact that crosslinking behavior initiated in the solvent hindered the crystallization of the PEO matrix. The decrease in crystallinity further affected the melting point (T
m ) and melting enthalpy (ΔH
m ) of HPE, DSC results showed that HPE had lower T
m and ΔH
m compared to SPE (Figure S13b). The absorption peaks representing the glass transition temperature (T
g ) at low temperatures shifted towards high temperatures (Figure S13c), indicating that the chain segment motion ability of PDA and PDACDs decreased in turn, which was closely related to the cross-linking form of polymers in HPE and further corresponded to the results of GPC. Based on the DSC results, the thermodynamic parameters of SPE and HPE were calculated and summarized in Table S2. The ionic conductivity of HPE was measured by assembling a symmetric battery with blocked electrodes. As shown in Figure S13d, PDACDs achieved the maximum ionic conductivity of 6.41×10−5 S·cm−1 at 30 °C with a NSFCDs addition of 3 wt%, while the ionic conductivity of SPE and PDA is 9.41×10−6 S·cm−1 and 1.47×10−5 S·cm−1, respectively. Considering the unusual relationship between the increase in ion conductivity of HPE and the increase in T
g , the ion conduction mechanism of HPE may not entirely depend on the relaxation effect of polymer segments, inter chain hopping may become the main approach of lithium ion conduction. Additional binding sites can be provided by N and S in NSFCDs for ion hopping, thereby promoting ion transport[22]. Based on this, Arrhenius formula was chosen for segmented fitting by plotting the logarithm of ion conductivity against the reciprocal of temperature (Figure S13e). The calculated activation energy (E
a ) of ion transport is shown in Figure S13f, at lower temperatures, SPE had the highest ion transport activation energy (0.339 eV) while PDA had the lowest (0.196 eV). As the temperature increased, the activation energy of all electrolytes decreased, but PDACDs reached the lowest activation energy (0.112 eV). This suggested that at lower temperatures, the conformation of polymers had a certain impact on ion conductivity, while above the melting point, this effect became less significant. At this point, the rich Lewis acid - base sites introduced by NSFCDs had a more remarkable impact on ion transport. The thermal stability of HPE was determined by TG (Figure S13g), significant mass loss occurred at temperatures above 350 °C. Specifically, the slightly higher decomposition temperature of SPE was attributed to its higher crystallinity[32]. PDACDs had a slightly lower decomposition temperature than PDA, which was influenced by the low thermal stability of NSFCDs and lower degree of polymerization. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) test (Figure S13h) and electrochemical flow test (Figure S13i) were conducted to evaluate the electrochemical stability of HPE. The LSV curves showed that SPE generated oxidation current when the voltage exceeded 4 V, while HPE had no apparent oxidation current before 5 V, reflecting the effective shielding effect of crosslinking reaction on the terminal hydroxyl groups of PEO[33]. Electrochemical flow test displayed that under constant voltage applied to the battery assembled with LiFePO4 (LFP) positive electrode material, the SPE exhibited a slight oxidation current at 4.5 V and continued to increase with voltage after exceeding 4.7 V. PDACDs undergo significant oxidation only after exceeding 4.8 V, demonstrating their superior electrochemical stability in real working environments. The electronic conductivity of electrolytes was tested by direct current (DC) polarization (Figure S14). The introduction of NSFCDs and construction of cross-linked polymer networks did not significantly affect the electronic conductivity of electrolytes, even with a certain degree of reduction, they were still insulated on this order of magnitude, thus avoiding the risk of breakdown.

In order to investigate the migration behavior of lithium ions within HPE, three bulk cases (system 1, system 2, system 3) (Figure S15) were built based on the original composition of these electrolytes for molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Fig. 4a-c displayed the schematic diagrams of the migration paths of lithium ions in these three models. It can be observed that compared to HPE (system 2 and system 3), system 1 displayed the more complex migration paths. The diffusion coefficients of lithium ions in the three systems can be measured based on the slope of the mean-azimuth shift curve by Einstein relationship equation[34]. As plotted in Fig. 4d-f, the calculated diffusion coefficients in SPE, PDA and PDACDs was 0.18383×10−5 cm2 s−1, 0.20323×10−5 cm2 s−1 and 0.25551×10−5 cm2 s−1, respectively. The result confirmed that lithium ions had the fastest diffusion rate in PDACDs, thereby enhancing ion conductivity. The coordination environment of lithium ions in electrolytes can be characterized by 7Li NMR spectra (Fig. 4e). The up-field shift of the 7Li signal in HPE indicated that the formation of cross-linked polymers increased the lithium ions conductivity by weakening the PEO - Li+ interaction[35], which was more significant in PDACDs. The ion transport in electrolytes was largely depended on the dissociation state of the salt, which was evaluated by Raman spectroscopy (Figure S16). The wavenumber region of 725–760 cm−1 was assigned to the TFSI- vibration[36], which can be fitted into two peaks located at 740 cm−1 and 746 cm−1 that corresponded to free ion and ion pair, respectively[37]. The content of free ions increased sequentially in SPE, PDA, and PDACDs, attributed to the introduction of carbonyl groups, which can induce charge delocalization of anions through solvent effects, thereby weakening the dissociation energy of lithium ions and promoting their diffusion rate[38]. Compared to PDA, the additional NSFCDs in PDACDs will enhance this effect, resulting in the production of more free ions.

Lithium ion transference (t
+ ) number was measured by alternating current (AC) impedance method combined with DC polarization (Figure S17). As reported in most works, conventional SPE composed of PEO and LiTFSI typically had lower t
+ (0.1273). Due to enhanced lithium salt dissociation and ion transport, PDA and PDACDs achieved t
+ of 0.3638 and 0.5833, respectively. The increased t
+ means that concentration polarization will be weakened during the lithium deposition process, thereby promoting uniform lithium deposition and alleviating lithium dendrites. In order to reveal the influence of different electrolytes on the lithium deposition process, Li/Cu batteries and Li symmetric batteries were assembled to evaluate the electroplating/stripping behavior and kinetics of lithium ions. The nucleation potential barrier of lithium deposition is closely related to the nucleation overpotential[39]. As shown in Fig. 4h, the initial nucleation overpotentials of SPE, PDA, and PDACDs were 168 mV, 113 mV, and 61 mV, respectively, indicating that PDACDs can regulate lithium deposition with lower nucleation barriers. The exchange current density (I
0 ) of lithium symmetric batteries is related to the ion transfer kinetics at the solid-state electrolyte interface (SEI)[40]. The I
0 calculated from the Tafel curves (Fig. 4i) were 11.2 μA·cm−2, 35.4 μA·cm−2 and 109.2 μA·cm−2 for SPE, PDA and PDACDs, respectively, further demonstrated that PDACDs had faster lithium ion transfer kinetics. In addition, the resulting SEI was more conducive to the migration of lithium ions.

Long-term Li plating and stripping tests were conducted to further evaluate the impact of HPE on lithium ion transport ( Fig. 5a). PDACDs exhibited good cycling stability when cycling at a current density of 0.1 mA·cm−2 for over 4000 h with minimal overpotential (76 mV), while SPE and PDA experienced short circuits at 173 h and 735 h, respectively. Satisfactory cycling stability was closely related to the construction of SEI on lithium metal surface. XPS sputtering was utilized to detect the lithium metal surface of lithium symmetric batteries after 100 h of cycling. As displayed in Fig. 5b and Figure S18, the surface SEI was mainly composed of TFSI- and carbon containing organic compounds, which can be revealed from the C 1 s, F 1 s, and S 2p spectra. It was difficult and confusing to fit the peaks of Li 1 s and N 1 s for component determination, as some components such as Li2CO3 and Li3N usually have similar binding energy, so peak fitting was not conducted. But it is worth noting that as the sputtering time increased, the peak of Li 1 s in SPE derived SEI shifted towards low binding energy (Figure S19), which was often attributed to organic lithium[41], Li2O, and Li0. A shift was also observed in the N 1 s spectra, where TFSI- with high binding energy (outer SEI) gradually transitioned towards Li3N with low binding energy (inner SEI).[42] No significant shift was observed in both PDA and PDACDs, indicating that the SEI derived from SPE was thinner, which can also be reflected in the peak intensity changes of LiF in F 1 s. The LiF intensity on the surface of SPE was higher than that of HPE, while the LiF content in the internal SEI showed a decreasing trend, which was likely due to the formation of uneven SEI leading to uneven lithium deposition. The higher proportion of TFSI- on the surface of PDACDs indicated a decrease in the degree of anion decomposition, while the internal LiF content did not decrease, which was attributed to the synergistic promotion of LiF formation by NSFCDs to stabilize the lithium metal interface. In the O 1s spectra, as the sputtering time increased, Li2O peaks (∼528 eV)[43] appeared in three electrolytes. Even if this was due to the decomposition of Li2CO3 during the sputtering process [44], it can indicate that the SEI derived from SPE had smaller thickness and more unstable internal composition. In the S 2p spectra, TFSI- and Li2SO3 signals were detected in the outer SEI of the electrolytes. As the sputtering time increased, the content of high valent sulfides gradually decreased, but it was still higher in HPE than in SPE, which can also be reflected in the O 1s spectra. The formation of Li2Sx, Li2S2, and Li2S was observed in the inner SEI. In addition, the peak position corresponding to organic polysulfides is close to Li2Sx, [45] while the peak intensity at this point in HPE was much greater than that in SPE. According to relevant reports [45], organic polysulfides were likely to originate from the co-deposition of sulfur containing groups in additives, which can be attributed to the role of NSFCDs. In contrast, SPE and PDA had more Li2S2 and Li2S components, which had been shown to have lower lithium ion conductivity. Therefore, the SEI derived from PDACDs had a more reasonable SEI composition, which included higher content of high valent sulfides, lower Li2S2 and Li2S, and organic polysulfides that had plasticized effects on SEI, thereby promoting the uniform deposition of lithium ions and alleviating volume changes. On the contrary, the SEI originating from SPE displayed thin and uneven features, which will cause the formation of dead lithium and more severe dendrite growth during cycling, consequently resulting in short circuits in the battery.

Considering the contribution of N, S, and F elements in TFSI- during the formation process of SEI, LiClO4 was selected as lithium salt to eliminate interference caused by anion decomposition of LiTFSI, and further evaluated the function of NSFCDs in SEI regulation. As shown in Figure S20, Figure S21 and Figure S22, as the sputtering time increased, a decrease in the peak intensity attributed to Li2CO3 can be observed in the C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of PDA, accompanied by an increase in peak intensity of Li2O. The results confirmed that the production of Li2O was closely related to the decomposition of Li2CO3. The decomposition phenomenon in PDACDs was significantly weaker than PDA, further indicating that PDACDs had more stable SEI components. In the C 1 s spectra of PDA, a peak associated with R (C) - Li was detected at the binding energy of 282.3 eV within the internal SEI, signifying a side reaction between electrolyte and lithium metal [46]. The resulting formation of metal carbides could compromise the stability of SEI and adversely impact the cycling process [47]. In contrast, no such substances were observed in the case of PDACDs. The results of S 2p, N 1s, and F 1s spectra showed that even after excluding the influence of decomposition products from TFSI-, the formation of LiF, Li3N, and various sulfides can still be observed, suggesting a significant involvement of NSFCDs in generating beneficial components for SEI. The decomposition products of ClO4 - can be analyzed through Cl 2p spectra. Cl containing substances can be divided into organic chlorides and LiCl, which can be observed in both PDA and PDACDs. It is noteworthy that the peak intensity of relevant substances in PDA diminished as the sputtering time increases, whereas the change in amplitude was smaller in PDACDs, which implied the non-uniform distribution of SEI components in PDA. Similar alteration in peak intensity of LiCl were also observed in the Li 1s spectra [48]. The more significant peak shift in PDA reflected the inadequate thickness resulting from the absence of SEI components, thus impacting the overall performance of SEI.

The mechanical properties of HPE play an important role in alleviating the volume strain of lithium metal and resisting dendrite piercing, which was investigated by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The average Young’s modulus of SPE, PDA and PDACDs was about 97 MPa, 234 MPa and 396 MPa, respectively ( Fig. 6a-c), indicating that the dense polymer network prepared through cross-linking polymerization strategy can effectively enhance the strain resistance of the polymer matrix. Analysis of the surface morphology of lithium metal after 100 h of cycling (Fig. 6d) revealed that the SPE derived lithium metal surface exhibited powdery and loose structure with cracks, while PDACDs showed a smoother and more uniform lithium deposition morphology, reflecting the excellent lithium ion conduction characteristics of PDACDs. To evaluate the electrochemical performance of HPE in full batteries, lithium metal batteries were assembled with LFP as the positive electrode material and subjected to long cycle and rate tests. As shown in Fig. 6e and f, all electrolytes were first activated at 0.1 C for 6 cycles. Considering the increasing capacity of the activation process, taking the first cycle of 0.5 C as the initial cycle, the full batteries with three electrolytes had initial specific capacities of 143.7 mAh·g−1, 142.1 mAh·g−1, and 155.9 mAh·g−1, respectively. After 200 cycles, the discharge capacity retention rate of PDACDs-based full battery reached 75.5 % and the coulombic efficiency almost remained at 99.5 %. While the capacity retention rate of SPE-based full battery was only 44.2 % and the coulombic efficiency was always below 99 %, which was due to the oxidative decomposition of SPE during charging process. Owing to the improved lithium ion conduction, PDACDs can reach the capacities of 153.8, 155.0, 156.4, 155.1, 143.0, 121.4 and 103.7 mAh·g−1 at 0.1 C, 0.2 C, 0.3 C, 0.5 C, 1 C, 2 C and 3 C, respectively. When the rate returned to 0.1 C, it can still reach a capacity of 150.6 mAh·g−1. In contrast, SPE and PDA exhibited poor rate performance, and SPE exhibited sustained capacity decay at rates exceeding 2 C. This result indicated that PDACDs had excellent cycle stability and rate performance under the studied working conditions, which can effectively improve the properties of all solid-state lithium metal batteries.

In summary, in order to improve the electrochemical performance of solid polymer electrolyte, we have designed and synthesized a new-type functional filler of carbon dots equipped with polyfunctional groups. By employing NSFCDs and PEGDA as additive and crosslinking agents, PEO-based hybrid polymer electrolyte was successfully obtained. A dense polymer network was constructed through free radical polymerization, effectively improving the mechanical properties of the polymer matrix. The lithium ion coordination environment has been improved through adjusting the polymer skeleton, promoting the rapid transport of lithium ions. The rational composition of SEI was regulated through NSFCDs in collaboration with anions, effectively alleviating the growth of lithium metal dendrites and volume strain. Thanks to the multifunctional contribution of NSFCDs, the prepared hybrid polymer electrolyte exhibited excellent properties, which can provide new insights for the design of polymer electrolytes.

Huaxin Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Laiqiang Xu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Fangjun Zhu: Methodology. Dingzhong Luo: Methodology. Yi Zhang: Resources. Wentao Deng: Funding acquisition. Guoqiang Zou: Funding acquisition. Hongshuai Hou: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiaobo Ji: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52074359, 22379165, U21A20284, U22B2069), Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2021RC3014), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University (2023ZZTS0068) and Hunan Provincial Innovation Foundation for Postgraduate (CX20230153).
